symptoms: Clinical signs typical of Tongue Injury
causes: Etiological agent related to Tongue Injury
Exposure to pathogens or dietary changes
detections.why_it_happened: The animal developed Tongue Injury due to direct exposure, pathogen accumulation, stress, or secondary complications.
prevention: Ensure proper vaccination and regular checkups
Avoid sudden dietary changes and contaminated feed
first_aid: Isolate the animal to prevent herd spread
Ensure a clean, dry, and sheltered recovery area
Provide fresh water and easily digestible feed
food_recommendations: Fresh green fodder and high-quality hay
Ensure adequate clean drinking water with electrolytes
hygiene_tips: Daily clean and disinfect the shed
Proper disposal of manure and contaminated bedding
Wash hands before and after treating the animal

detections.why_it_happened: The animal is in excellent health due to proper care, good hygiene, and a well-balanced diet.
food_recommendations: Balanced ratio of green and dry fodder
Concentrate feed based on milk yield
Access to fresh, clean drinking water

symptoms: Loss of appetite and weight loss
detections.why_it_happened: The animal likely came in contact with the FMD virus through infected animals, contaminated feed, or was exposed to airborne virus particles. Poor quarantine practices increase the risk.
prevention: Restrict visitor access to the farm
medicine: Anti-inflammatory drugs (Meloxicam) (Dosage: 0.5 mg/kg, Freq: Once daily, Dur: 3 days)
food_recommendations: Green fodder cut into small pieces
hygiene_tips: Disinfect the shed daily with phenol
Keep flies away with neem spray

symptoms: Firm round skin nodules (2–5 cm diameter)
causes: Spread by mosquitoes and biting flies
detections.why_it_happened: The virus was transmitted through insect bites (mosquitoes, flies) or direct contact with infected animals. Lack of vaccination and poor vector control contributed.
prevention: Annual vaccination with live attenuated vaccine
Insect control using sprays and nets
medicine: Antibiotics for secondary infections (Oxytetracycline) (Dosage: 10 mg/kg, Freq: Every 24 hours, Dur: 5 days)
first_aid: Provide supportive care — fluids and shade
hygiene_tips: Use insect repellent around the shed
Separate healthy animals from sick ones

symptoms: Raised skin nodules and pox lesions
detections.why_it_happened: The virus was transmitted through insect bites or direct contact with infected animals. Lack of vaccination and poor hygiene in shelters contributed to the outbreak.

Keep animal in clean, ventilated area

symptoms: Firm round skin nodules (2–5 cm diameter)
causes: Spread by mosquitoes and biting flies

symptoms: Intense itching and rubbing against fences
Thickened, wrinkled skin in chronic cases
causes: Mites survive off-host for up to 17 days
detections.why_it_happened: Psoroptes ovis mites transferred from infected sheep through direct contact or shared equipment. The mites multiply rapidly under the wool, causing intense irritation.
prevention: Inspect flock regularly for signs of itching
first_aid: Treat the entire flock within 14 days
Report to veterinary authority if required
hygiene_tips: Clean and disinfect all pens after treatment
Leave pens empty for 17+ days if possible
Wash all shearing equipment between animals

detections.why_it_happened: The goat developed Neonatal Diarrhea due to direct exposure, physiological susceptibility, or environmental conditions.
prevention: Maintain clean, dry, and well-ventilated goat housing

Wash hands thoroughly before and after handling the animals

symptoms: Scabby sores around mouth and lips
Weight loss due to reduced feed intake
Lesions on udder in lactating does
causes: Virus survives in scabs for months
detections.why_it_happened: The parapoxvirus spread through contaminated feed troughs or direct contact. Rough feed can cause small mouth cuts that allow virus entry.
prevention: Vaccinate kids at 1–2 months of age
Disinfect feed and water troughs regularly
medicine: Antibiotics only if secondary infection present
first_aid: Clean sores gently with warm saline
Hand-feed soft food if animal cannot eat
Wear gloves — this disease can spread to humans
food_recommendations: Soft, moist feed that is easy to chew
hygiene_tips: Always wear gloves when treating (zoonotic disease)
Burn or bury scabs — do not leave on ground

chat_messages.text: Tell me about Jaw Swelling in goats
chat_messages.text_en: Tell me about Jaw Swelling in goats

chat_messages.text:
**Disease Identified:** Jaw Swelling (in Goat)
**Severity:** Moderate

**Symptoms:**
- Enlargement of jaw region
- Pain while chewing

**First Aid:**
- Isolate the goat to ensure easy access to feed
- Provide soft, non-abrasive, easily chewable feed
- Consult a veterinarian immediately

**Recommended Medicine:**
- Antibiotics (Penicillin/Streptomycin under vet advice)
- Anti-inflammatory drugs

**Prevention:**
- Avoid feeding extremely coarse or thorny forage
- Regularly inspect teeth and mouth for injuries
- Maintain clean feeding troughs

chat_messages.text: Uploaded image for Sheep diagnosis
chat_messages.text_en: Uploaded image for Sheep diagnosis

chat_messages.text:
I have analyzed your image. It looks like your Sheep has **Sheep Scab (Psoroptic Mange)** (Confidence level: 82%).

**Severity:** Moderate

**Symptoms:**
- Intense itching and rubbing against fences
- Wool loss in patches
- Yellow crusty scabs on skin
- Restlessness and weight loss
- Thickened, wrinkled skin in chronic cases

**First Aid:**
- Separate affected sheep from flock
- Apply approved acaricide treatment
- Treat the entire flock within 14 days
- Report to veterinary authority if required

**Suggested Medicine:**
- Ivermectin injection (two doses 7 days apart)
- Organophosphorus dip (full immersion)
- Moxidectin pour-on
- Doramectin injection
chat_messages.text_en:

**Severity:** Moderate

- Intense itching and rubbing against fences

chat_messages.text: Uploaded image for Goat diagnosis
chat_messages.text_en: Uploaded image for Goat diagnosis

chat_messages.text:
I have analyzed your image. It looks like your Goat has **Goat Pox** (Confidence level: 94.36%).

**Severity:** High

**Symptoms:**
- Raised skin nodules and pox lesions
- High fever (41–42°C)
- Nasal and eye discharge
- Swollen lymph nodes
- Difficulty breathing

**First Aid:**
- Isolate affected animals
- Apply Povidone-iodine on lesions
- Provide supportive care — fluids and nutrition
- Keep animal in clean, ventilated area

**Suggested Medicine:**
- Antibiotics for secondary infections (Amoxicillin)
- Antiseptic ointment on pox lesions
- Anti-inflammatory drugs
- Vitamin supplements for recovery

chat_messages.text: What causes PPR in goats?
chat_messages.text_en: What causes PPR in goats?

chat_messages.text:
Main causes of **Peste des Petits Ruminants (PPR)** (in Goat):
- PPR virus (Morbillivirus family)
- Direct contact with infected animals
- Contaminated water and feed troughs
- Aerosol transmission in crowded conditions

**Why it happened:**
The virus spread through direct contact with infected goats or contaminated feed/water. Crowded housing and poor ventilation accelerated transmission.

- Aerosol transmission in crowded conditions

**Why it happened:**
The virus spread through direct contact with infected goats or contaminated feed/water. Crowded housing and poor ventilation accelerated transmission.

chat_messages.text: What are the symptoms of Lumpy Skin?
chat_messages.text_en: What are the symptoms of Lumpy Skin?

chat_messages.text:
The main symptoms of **Lumpy Skin Disease** (in Cow) are:

- Fever
- Skin nodules
- Firm round skin nodules (2–5 cm diameter)
- High fever above 41°C
- Swollen lymph nodes
- Reduced milk yield
- Nasal and eye discharge

chat_messages.text: ಲಂಪಿ ಚರ್ಮ ರೋಗದ ಲಕ್ಷಣಗಳು ಯಾವುವು?
chat_messages.text_en: ಲಂಪಿ ಚರ್ಮ ರೋಗದ ಲಕ್ಷಣಗಳು ಯಾವುವು?

chat_messages.text:
**ಗಂಟು ಚರ್ಮ ರೋಗ** (ಪ್ರಾಣಿ: ಹಸು) ನ ಮುಖ್ಯ ರೋಗಲಕ್ಷಣಗಳು ಈ ಕೆಳಗಿನಂತಿವೆ:

- ಜ್ವರ
- ಚರ್ಮದ ಗಂಟುಗಳು
- ದೃಢವಾದ ಸುತ್ತಿನ ಚರ್ಮದ ಗಂಟುಗಳು (2-5 ಸೆಂ ವ್ಯಾಸ)
- 41 ° C ಗಿಂತ ಹೆಚ್ಚಿನ ಜ್ವರ
- ಊದಿಕೊಂಡ ದುಗ್ಧರಸ ಗ್ರಂಥಿಗಳು
- ಹಾಲಿನ ಇಳುವರಿ ಕಡಿಮೆಯಾಗಿದೆ
- ಮೂಗು ಮತ್ತು ಕಣ್ಣಿನ ಡಿಸ್ಚಾರ್ಜ್
chat_messages.text_en:
The main symptoms of **Lumpy Skin Disease** (in Cow) are:

- Fever
- Skin nodules
- Firm round skin nodules (2–5 cm diameter)
- High fever above 41°C
- Swollen lymph nodes
- Reduced milk yield
- Nasal and eye discharge

chat_messages.text: ಆಡುಗಳಲ್ಲಿ ಪಿಪಿಆರ್ ರೋಗಕ್ಕೆ ಕಾರಣವೇನು?
chat_messages.text_en: ಆಡುಗಳಲ್ಲಿ ಪಿಪಿಆರ್ ರೋಗಕ್ಕೆ ಕಾರಣವೇನು?

chat_messages.text:
**ಕುರಿ ಮತ್ತು ಆಡುಗಳ ಪಿಪಿಆರ್ ರೋಗ** (ಪ್ರಾಣಿ: ಆಡು) ತಗಲಲು ಮುಖ್ಯ ಕಾರಣಗಳು:
- PPR ವೈರಸ್ (ಮಾರ್ಬಿಲ್ಲಿವೈರಸ್ ಕುಟುಂಬ)
- ಸೋಂಕಿತ ಪ್ರಾಣಿಗಳೊಂದಿಗೆ ನೇರ ಸಂಪರ್ಕ
- ಕಲುಷಿತ ನೀರು ಮತ್ತು ಆಹಾರ ತೊಟ್ಟಿಗಳು
- ಕಿಕ್ಕಿರಿದ ಪರಿಸ್ಥಿತಿಗಳಲ್ಲಿ ಏರೋಸಾಲ್ ಪ್ರಸರಣ

**ಇದು ಏಕೆ ಸಂಭವಿಸಿದೆ:**
ಸೋಂಕಿತ ಮೇಕೆಗಳು ಅಥವಾ ಕಲುಷಿತ ಆಹಾರ/ನೀರಿನೊಂದಿಗೆ ನೇರ ಸಂಪರ್ಕದ ಮೂಲಕ ವೈರಸ್ ಹರಡುತ್ತದೆ. ಕಿಕ್ಕಿರಿದ ವಸತಿ ಮತ್ತು ಕಳಪೆ ವಾತಾಯನ ಪ್ರಸರಣವನ್ನು ವೇಗಗೊಳಿಸಿತು.
chat_messages.text_en:
Main causes of **Peste des Petits Ruminants (PPR)** (in Goat):
- PPR virus (Morbillivirus family)
- Direct contact with infected animals
- Contaminated water and feed troughs
- Aerosol transmission in crowded conditions

**Why it happened:**
The virus spread through direct contact with infected goats or contaminated feed/water. Crowded housing and poor ventilation accelerated transmission.

chat_messages.text: बकरियों में पीपीआर रोग का क्या कारण है?
chat_messages.text_en: बकरियों में पीपीआर रोग का क्या कारण है?

chat_messages.text:
**पीपीआर रोग (बकरी प्लेग)** (पशु: बकरी) के मुख्य कारण:
- पीपीआर वायरस (मॉर्बिलीवायरस परिवार)
- संक्रमित जानवरों से सीधा संपर्क
- दूषित पानी और चारा कुंड
- भीड़-भाड़ वाली परिस्थितियों में एरोसोल संचरण

**यह क्यों हुआ:**
यह वायरस संक्रमित बकरियों या दूषित चारे/पानी के सीधे संपर्क से फैलता है। भीड़भाड़ वाले आवास और खराब वेंटिलेशन के कारण संचरण में तेजी आई।
